coredata.dc:description: In the recent past, agrotechnology has witnessed a tremendous change due to the advent of technologies employed towards enhancing the yield and quality of the crops. The application of nanotechnology in agriculture is promoting sustainable practices and smart farming. Currently, nanomaterial-based sensors are gaining considerable attention in agrotechnology, such sensors can detect the environmental conditions and support for crop growth and enhances the agricultural yield. Graphiticcarbon nitride (g-C3N4), a soft polymer belongs to a class of 2-D semiconductors which are affordable, metal-free, indispensable for sensing applications due to significant changes in their conductivity upon exposure to analyte molecules in nano-regime. Further, this g-C3N4 can be combined with other nano metal oxides to form nanocomposites with remarkable properties and superior performance. In this work we report development of high-performance sensors based on graphitic carbon nitride embedded with copper and iron oxide nanoparticles. These heterostructures offers tremendous improvement in the physical and chemical properties due to the synergetic interactions between various phases. The chemiresistive sensor fabricated from these nanocomposites exhibit excellent sensitivity towards monitoring methane gas @ ppb level, relative humidity (% RH), and soil moisture content. Owing to their excellent sensitivity, stability and selectivity, this graphitic carbon nitride embedded with Cu and Fe oxide nanoparticles could be potentially used as multifunctional sensors towards environmental monitoring and greenhouse conditions in agricultural applications.

head.ce:title: Design and development of multi-functional graphitic carbon nitride heterostructures embedded with copper and iron oxide nanoparticles as versatile sensing platforms for environmental and agricultural applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the recent past, agrotechnology has witnessed a tremendous change due to the advent of technologies employed towards enhancing the yield and quality of the crops. The application of nanotechnology in agriculture is promoting sustainable practices and smart farming. Currently, nanomaterial-based sensors are gaining considerable attention in agrotechnology, such sensors can detect the environmental conditions and support for crop growth and enhances the agricultural yield. Graphiticcarbon nitride (g-C3N4), a soft polymer belongs to a class of 2-D semiconductors which are affordable, metal-free, indispensable for sensing applications due to significant changes in their conductivity upon exposure to analyte molecules in nano-regime. Further, this g-C3N4 can be combined with other nano metal oxides to form nanocomposites with remarkable properties and superior performance. In this work we report development of high-performance sensors based on graphitic carbon nitride embedded with copper and iron oxide nanoparticles. These heterostructures offers tremendous improvement in the physical and chemical properties due to the synergetic interactions between various phases. The chemiresistive sensor fabricated from these nanocomposites exhibit excellent sensitivity towards monitoring methane gas @ ppb level, relative humidity (% RH), and soil moisture content. Owing to their excellent sensitivity, stability and selectivity, this graphitic carbon nitride embedded with Cu and Fe oxide nanoparticles could be potentially used as multifunctional sensors towards environmental monitoring and greenhouse conditions in agricultural applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Progress in agricultural sciences and technology are crucial for improving the production efficiency and sustainability towards the smart agricultural practices. The greenhouse contributes towards significant production of agricultural products such as fruits and vegetables, there is a steady increase in crop production via greenhouse methods due to less available of fertile land [1]. One of the significant importance of greenhouse farming is that they offer huge advantage of round-the-year production, facilitating the financial stability for the farmers [2]. During the plant growth process and product storage in agricultural greenhouse environments, number of factors that affects the growth/production quality and prolonged shelf life of grown crops, such as humidity, soil moisture content, temperature, and organic volatiles [3–5]. Monitoring such parameters can be achieved with smart and high-performance sensors that typically require the use of advanced sensing materials [6]. Sensors are of three kinds based on response of the sensing materials: (1) chemical sensors-refers to analyte interaction with the sensing material through chemical responses, (2) physical sensors-that measures a change in physical quantity and (3) biosensors-where a biosensing element is used to detect the chemical/physical changes [7]. Some of the sensing materials reported in the past mostly belongs to the classes of metal oxides [8–10], polymers (both conducting and non-conducting) [11–14], and carbon-based materials (carbon nanotubes and graphene) [15–17]. Also, there are reports on heterostructure sensing materials, such as metal oxide/CNTs composites [18], composites containing polymer-graphene materials [19], polymer nanocomposites, ionic liquids [20] and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [21]. The sensing materials that have been developed in the past decade for the detection of various analytes are complex to synthesize with expensive starting materials, which hinders their large-scale production and fabrication of commercially viable greenhouse detector. It is noticed that many of these sensing materials demonstrate explicit sensitivity and selectivity to certain kind of target analytes, hence there is a growing need to develop smart multifunctional sensing materials for the detection of environment in agricultural greenhouses [2].

Advances in the field of nanotechnology have led to the design and development of nanomaterials with unique physical/chemical properties, functionalities that can be tailored to suit a particular application, morphologies, aspect ratios, tunable band gaps, and so on. These interesting properties of nanomaterials have been extensively investigated by material scientists across the world to explore their potential applications in the field of electronics, catalysis, energy conversion, sensing, bioimaging and photonics [22–29]. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a stable 2D allotrope belongs to the family of carbon nitride that emerged as highly promising candidate for many of the technological applications due to ease of source material availability, purest form, inexpensive synthesis, ease of structural modifications, chemical and thermal stability [30–32]. Moreover, g-C3N4 and its composites have been extensively used in catalysis (for oxygen reduction, photocatalysis, and water splitting agents), photocathodes, as light-emitting diodes (LED), imaging and sensing, owing to their unique physical/chemical properties, abundant active sites, large surface area, and nanoarchitectured morphology [33–38]. Very recently, rapid growth in the design and development of g-C3N4 based nanomaterials has flourished, and these materials are extensively used for the fabrication of various electroanalytical devices [39–43]. Due to several interesting features (such as high charge density and charge transfer characteristics, stability, superior fluorescence quenching, opto-electronic conversion capability, excellent biocompatibility) that are essential for sensing applications, these nanoarchitectured g-C3N4 were utilized as a promising material for the fabrication sensor devices to detect various analytes [43].

Due to increase in multiple complexities towards agricultural monitoring, there is a growing need towards the simultaneous detection of multiple analytes at one stretch. To achieve simultaneous detection of multiple analytes using a single sensing material, g-C3N4 morphology needs to be modified with the formation of suitable composite structures. More recently, heterostructures containing g-C3N4 and oxides of iron [44,45] or copper [45,46] have attracted considerable interest as they are earth-abundant and can facilitate production of low-cost hybrid material. Importantly, integrating g-C3N4 with such oxide nanoparticles (NPs) can result in stable material with excellent charge transport [47]. Even though there are some reports on catalytic performance of g-C3N4 with Cu/Fe mixed metal oxide composites, however, to date, no report is available on exploring the sensing performance of these heterostructures especially towards detection of multiple analytes in greenhouse agricultural applications.

Herein, we report the synthesis of g-C3N4 based heterostructure nanocomposites using Cu/Fe mixed metal oxides in two steps, a thermal decomposition polymerization followed by a hydrothermal synthesis. This heterostructure nanocomposite is further used to fabricate a sensor device to detect multiple analytes (such as gas, humidity, and soil moisture content) in greenhouse agricultural applications. The synergistic effects in the heterostructure leads to significant enhancement in electrically active surface area and the oxygen deficiencies in Cu/Fe mixed metal oxides that improves the kinetics of the electron transfer at the surface. Due to its low-cost synthesis, excellent sensing performance, long term stability and selectivity these novel g-C3N4 based heterostructure nanocomposites with Cu/Fe mixed metal oxides can be potentially used as chemiresistive sensors in detection of multiple analytes in greenhouse agricultural applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nano graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) and GCN-nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) was carried out through thermal decomposition method as reported earlier [15]. In a typical procedure described earlier, 2 gm of melamine was dissolved in 5 mL of distilled water and kept overnight at 80 °C. The resulting powder after filtration was placed in the muffle furnace and heated for 2 h at 550 °C with a ramping time of 5 °C/min. After cooling down, obtained a yellow-colored graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) that was grounded finely and used for further experimental studies. As prepared g-C3N4 was converted into thin nano g-C3N4 sheets as reported earlier [48] with slight modifications. Wherein (1 gm) of g-C3N4 was mixed with 10 mL of H2SO4 (98 wt %) in a round-bottom flask and continuously stirred for 12 h at room temperature under dark conditions. The mixture was then transferred into beaker containing 50 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 2 h to facilitate exfoliation. The resulting suspension was then centrifuged, collected solid was thoroughly washed with deionized water followed by drying at 50 °C in a vacuum oven overnight. The resulting powder (1 gm) was added in a flask containing 250 mL of methanol and refluxed in an oil bath heater at 60 °C for 6 h. Further centrifuged and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven. This final product of nano g-C3N4 is labelled as GCN for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu mixed metal oxide nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The GCN-Iron oxide (GCN-Fe) and GCN-Iron oxide-Copper oxide (GCN-Fe-Cu) mixed metal oxide nanocomposites were synthesized by hydrothermal method using the procedures described before [45,49]. In a typical procedure, 5 gm of GCN powder synthesized above, was dissolved in a 25 mL distilled water followed by sonication for 30 min. For the synthesis of GCN-Fe-Cu, 3.168 gm of FeCl3.6H2O, 1 gm of FeCl2, 1 gm of CuCl2 and 100 mg of urea were added into the GCN suspension with sonication for 40 min. During the sonication, liquid ammonia (NH3) was added dropwise to the above solution mixture to change its pH to 10 and further stirred for 30 min. The resulting solution was then transferred to a stainless-steel autoclave and kept for 12 h at 180 °C. Further, the reaction mixture was allowed to cool down, when it reaches the room temperature, resulting precipitate of GCN-Fe-Cu was washed with ethanol and distilled water several times and dried at 80 °C under dynamic vacuum. This heterostructure composite is labelled as GCN-Fe-Cu for further studies. The GCN-Iron Oxide nanocomposite was synthesized similarly as discussed above without the addition of copper chloride and is labelled as GCN-Fe for further studies.

The surface morphology of GCN, GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposites were investigated using High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEOL JEM, 2100) operated at a voltage of 200 kV. Crystallographic structure of the synthesized samples was analyzed through X-ray diffractometer (Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.540598 Å). FT-IR spectra of all the samples was recorded (Bruker Tensor 27) in the wavenumber range 4000–500 cm−1. The chemical analysis and elemental composition of GCN, GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposites was studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (K-alpha Surface Analysis, Thermo Scientific) with Al Kα radiation as an X-ray source operating at room temperature.

0.5 gm of GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposite heterostructure obtained by hydrothermal synthesis as discussed above was ultrasonicated in deionized water for 30 min to obtain uniform dispersion. ITO coated glass substrates were cleaned in ultrasonic baths followed by using detergent (TFD4), deionized water, acetone, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and dried for 1 h at 100 °C in an oven. The ITO coated substrates were then treated by flowing nitrogen followed by oxygen plasma (1 Torr for 10 min) to make the substrates more hydrophilic. The dispersion of GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposite was spin coated onto pre-treated (hydrophilic) ITO glass slides to obtain a thin film. The spin coated thin film on ITO coated glass was dried at dynamic vacuum @ 80 °C for 2 h. An array of gold electrodes was deposited onto the sensing film using mark etching followed by deposition of silver contact electrodes by thermal deposition method to obtain a final sensing device as shown in Fig. 1 of schematic representation. GCN and GCN-Fe sensors were fabricated for comparison in similar procedures discussed above.

The sensor characteristics were investigated for bare GCN, GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu using a laboratory made gas and humidity sensing set up designed and reported in our previous studies [3,50]. The schematic diagrams of sensor set up for both gas and humidity sensors are provided in supplementary information-S1. The standard protocols for the calibration of sensor setup were followed as discussed in our reports [3,50]. Methane sensing response of GCN, GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposites is monitored at room temperature in a sealed borosilicate cylindrical chamber of volume 250 cm3 (as indicated in figure-A supplementary information-S1). Mass flow meters (Fusion Flow-Gas mixing systems) were used to control the flow of inlet test gases (concentrations) around the sensing film. Keithley electrical probe station (6487 pico ammeter/voltmeter system) with LabView graphic interface to collect the real-time data of the sensing films at a biasing voltage of 2 V. The sensor response of a chemiresistive sensor is evaluated based on the change in resistance of the sensing films, and the sensor response of the film is calculated using the relation

Where, Ra and Rg are the resistances of the sensing films in air and gas, respectively. The response time of the sensing films in the presence of methane gas is obtained as time required attaining 90% of the change in base resistance (when the gas is ON) and whereas the recovery time is to attain equilibrium (when the gas flow is switched off).

The humidity sensing response of GCN, GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposites is investigated at room temperature in a laboratory designed humidity sensor setup (shown in supplementary information S1-Figure-B) by measuring the change in resistance. The %RH in the sensor setup around the test samples is controlled by humidifiers and monitored by hygrometers. The resistance change for different %RH is recorded by using Keithley electric probe station mentioned above with LabView graphic interface to collect the real-time data of the sensing films at a biasing voltage of 2 V. The response of sensing film for different %RH is evaluated using the relation

Where, RA and RH are the resistances of the sensing films in dry air and humidity, respectively. The response time is calculated as time to reach 90% change in baseline resistance in the presence of humidity and recovery time is to attain equilibrium in the absence of humidity. Similarly, the soil moisture content is investigated for GCN, GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposites for both black and red soils by measuring the change in resistance of the samples by using Keithley electric workstation discussed above. The sensor response for soil moisture content (for different gravimetric volume content of water in soil) was investigated using the relation

Where, RDry and RWet are the resistances of the sensing films in dry soil and wet soil, respectively. The response and recovery time are calculated using the similar procedures mentioned above to attain 90% of base line resistance in the presence of water content in the soil and to attain equilibrium (in the absence of water content in soil) respectively.
Initially, the sensing response of the sensing films were calibrated and optimized by passing test gas to ensure linearity of the sensing response followed by saturation at maxima.

Morphological features and elemental mapping of GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposites were carried out using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and EDAX mapping. The HR-TEM images (Fig. 2 ) of GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu shows thin transparent sheet like features for GCN having voids and decorated with FeO and CuO nanoparticles. The nanocomposite exhibits well distributed FeO and CuO nanoparticles on the GCN interfacial layers confirms the formation of nano heterostructure composite. The interplanar spacings (d-spacing) between two domains in GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposite is measured using HR-TEM which is about 0.2834 nm which is in close agreement with the previous literature [45,51]. The elemental mapping and energy dispersive analysis (EDAX) of heterostructure nanocomposite (Fig. 2) shows well distributed carbon and nitrogen in host GCN. Further the host GCN has well dispersed oxygen, Fe and Cu nanoparticles. The uniform distribution of Fe, Cu and oxygen in the elemental mapping supports the formation of heterostructure nanocomposite involving GCN, FeO and CuO. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) images of GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposite indicates concentric circles with uniform distribution of high intensity spots, indicating polycrystalline nature for the heterostructure. The HR-TEM, EDAX and SAED analysis reflects the formation GCN-Fe-Cu heterostructure with well dispersed FeO and CuO nanoparticles in GCN.

The chemical environments of the surface for GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposites were analyzed through X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) and are depicted in Fig. 3 (a–f). The high resolution XPS survey scan of GCN-Fe-Cu heterostructure surface indicated in Fig. 3a shows the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, iron, and copper elements at the surface, whereas the Fig. 3 (b–f) shows the composed transitions from different elements present in GCN-Fe-Cu composite and their peak deconvolution analysis. The binding energy oxidation state and their corresponding interactions for C 1s (Fig. 3b) results into peaks with binding energies 284.8 and 288 eV were due to standard reference carbon and N=C–N and C=N/C=C respectively [51]. The prominent peak at 398.3 eV for N 1s (Fig. 3c) is attributed due to the presence of two sp2 coordinate bonded N (C=N–C) atoms involved in the heptazine rings, whereas the peak at 398.3 eV corresponds to the N atoms bonding with three carbon atoms in GCN [52]. Fig. 3d shows O 1s spectra with peak corresponds to binding energy (BE) at 530.2 eV was ascribed to O 1s of metal oxides [53]. The peak at BE 531.9 eV for O 1s was attributed to O2 adsorbed on oxygen deficiency site [54] that may enhance the overall sensing performance of the composites. The binding energy oxidation state and their corresponding interactions for Fe 2Ps shown in Fig. 3e displays characteristic peak shown at BE corresponding to 712.5 eV was attributed to ferrous 2p3/2 photoelectron peak of ferric species [55], whereas the weak peak at 722.5 eV corresponds to 2p1/2 of ferric species [55]. The binding energy oxidation state and their corresponding interactions for Cu 2Ps in Fig. 3f at BE 932.9 eV refers to Cu 2p3/2 of Cu+ and 953.2 eV refers to weak 2p1/2 arising due to partial oxidation of CuO respectively. Hence, the detailed chemical survey scan of GCN-Fe-Cu heterostructure confirms the successful formation of composite.

The structural features of GCN, GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu heterostructures were analyzed using X-Ray Diffractograms and represented in Fig. 4 (A) . The XRD pattern of pristine GCN shows two distinct peaks at 2θ values of 13.5 and 27.6, corresponds to (100) and (002) characteristic planes of GCN as reported by us earlier [15]. The broad characteristic peak confirms the semi-crystalline nature of GCN. The X-Ray diffractogram of GCN-Fe shows intense diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 30.2, 36.5, 38, 42.91, 54.8, 57.3, 63.21, corresponds to the crystal planes (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (2 2 2), (4 0 0), (2 0 0), (5 1 1), (4 4 0) of iron oxide NPs (JCPDS Card No: 19–629 and 39–1346). These XRD result indicates the spinal structure of iron oxide NPs [56]. The peaks corresponding to diffractogram of GCN-Fe-Cu at 2θ values of 33.5, 36.2, and 47.4 belongs to (110), (002), (022) and (2 0 2) planes of CuO NPs (JCPDS Card No. 48–1548). The X-Ray diffractogram of GCN-Fe-Cu heterostructure also indicates the retention of major characteristic peaks of FeO such as (3 1 1), (4 0 0), (2 0 0), (5 1 1), (4 4 0) in the composite. The intensities of the GCN peak have significantly decreased after the incorporation of FeO and CuO in GCN. Results of XRD analysis clearly indicates successful formation of heterostructure based on GCN and mixed metal oxide which agrees with EDAX mapping, XPS and SAED analysis.

For the assessment of chemical bonding among the synthesized heterostructures, FT-IR studies were performed and are depicted in Fig. 4 (B). The broad characteristic adsorption peak in the wavenumber 3150–3390 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration modes of N–H bonds related to primary and secondary amines in GCN [Fig. 4(B)-a]. The sharp intense peaks at 1648 cm−1 and 1323 cm−1 corresponds to stretching modes of C–N (C=N and C–N) heterocycles in GCN [15]. The peak at 809 cm−1 is attributed to the breathing ring vibrational mode of the s-triazine unit present in the GCN (carbon nitride system). The FT-IR spectra corresponding to GCN-Fe [Fig. 4(B)-b] shows broad characteristic peak at 3472 cm−1 which results due N–H vibration modes of GCN and O–H stretching modes of Iron oxide NPs [57]. The peak at 1630 cm−1 results due to interactions of C–N stretching modes of heterocycles in GCN and O–H stretching modes of Iron oxide NPs. The peaks correspond to 1406 cm−1, 1120 cm−1 and 616 cm−1 results due to flexural vibrations of Fe–O bonds [57]. The FTIR spectrum of GCN-Fe-Cu [Fig. 4(B)-c] reveals similar pattern like GCN-Fe with additional peaks arising due to stretching vibrations of Cu–O bonds at 1652 cm−1 [58]. The composite FTIR spectrum of GCN-Fe-Cu heterostructure demonstrates retention of major characteristic peaks of GCN, FeO and CuO with slight blue shift, which indicates synergetic interactions between different phases and successful formation of composite structure. The increase in intensity of peaks in composite spectra compared to pristine GCN also indicates presence of strong interactions between GCN, FeO and CuO phases.

Materials with enhanced conductivity plays a prominent role as chemiresistive sensors towards detection of various analytes. The variation of electrical conductivity as a function of temperature for GCN, GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu films studied at room temperature and are illustrated in Fig. 5 . It is interesting to note that the conductivity of GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu heterostructures significantly enhanced in comparison to bare GCN. The increase in the conductivity for GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu is more pronounced at higher temperatures indicating thermally assisted hopping of charge carriers in heterostructures. The intercalation of FeO and CuO nanoparticles in GCN layers leads to increased charge carrier density and formation of conducting islands in the composite structure. These conducting island generates large number of π–π delocalized electrons in the GCN-Fe-Cu composite and facilitate the ease of charge carrier transport leading to enhanced conductivity. Further, detailed understanding about the mechanism of charge carrier transport and enhanced conductivity in these heterostructures needs to be understood in depth. The GCN-Fe-Cu heterostructure shows conductivity of a 195 S/cm in compare pure GCN thin films about 18 S/cm @ 300 °C. The improved conductivity of GCN-Fe-Cu heterostructure plays a significant role in high performance sensing characteristics of the material.

The methane gas sensing response @ ppb level for pure GCN, GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposite heterostructures were investigated by fabricating a sensing device as discussed in the experimental section. The change in gas sensing response as a function of methane gas concentration (10–1000 ppb) for pure GCN, GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu films investigated at ambient temperature were illustrated in the Fig. 6 a. The gas sensing responses is almost linear at lower concentration of gas up to the 300 ppb and the sensing response gradually increases at higher gas concentrations. It is observed from the plots, that the gas sensing response was found to be maximum for GCN-Fe-Cu film which is about 90% in comparison to the pure GCN which is about 30% upon exposure of methane gas. The increase in sensitivity of GCN with methane concentration could be attributed to receptor functionalities. When the sample was exposed to air, atmospheric oxygen gets adsorbed on the surface and ionized to O
−
2 , that traps the electrons and forming the depletion region at the n-type material surface. Since GCN behaves as n-type material its resistance in the presence of air increases. When the nanocomposites are exposed to methane gas, in the presence of reducing gas such as methane [CH4] its resistance decreases as per the reaction mechanism.

4
]
g a s + 2
[
O 2 −

( a d s o r b e d )

2

H 2

The electrons are being released into metal oxides during the reaction, contributes to reduction in the resistance of the composite structures. The overall increase in sensing response of GCN composites in the presence of methane could be potentially due to response of metal oxides to change in resistance via oxygen absorption and desorption mechanisms. Further the increased sensitivity of GCN-Fe could be attributed to the formation of n-n heterojunctions between GCN and FeO, whereas the formation of n-p-n heterojunctions substantially increases the sensitivity in GCN-Fe-Cu composites as CuO behaves like p-type material. The heterojunctions also expected to enhance the charge carrier densities at the interface between GCN and metal oxide NPs. The presence of metal oxide NPs in GCN also expected to increase the oxygen active sites on the composite surface which facilitates excess release of electrons when exposed to methane gas analytes leading to improved sensitivities. The resistance change shows strong dependance on the methane concentration, hence the sensitivity also increases with change in methane concentrations due to release of excessive electrons.

Repeatability is one of the important parameters for commercial and technological applications of sensors. The variation of real time dynamic response in terms of change in resistance at 1000 ppb of methane gas for GCN-Fe-Cu film sensor studied at room temperature as illustrated in the Fig. 6b. The dynamic response of the heterostructure indicates that, the fabricated sensor is highly stable to different cycles of operation. Further, the selectivity studies were conducted (average of 3 trials to ensure reproducibility) using this GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposite heterostructure and depicted in Fig. 6c. The selectivity studies confirms that the fabricated sensor is highly sensitive to methane gas in comparison to other test gases. The long-term stability of the GCN-Fe-Cu film sensor studied at room temperature for 1000 ppb of methane gas is illustrated in Fig. 6d. It is interesting to note that the GCN-Fe-Cu sensor shows highly stable sensing response even after 30 days, which signifies the practical usability of the sensor over a long period of time. Further, the fabricated sensor shows very short response and recovery time of 42 s and 51s (table-1 of supplementary information-S2) respectively for 1000 ppb methane gas. Excellent methane sensing response, good selectivity, long term stability and short response/recovery time makes these GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposite as a suitable material for the detection of greenhouse methane gas at ppb concentrations.

Fig. 7 a shows the variation of resistance for GCN, GCN-Fe, and GCN-Fe-Cu film sensor to different RH levels. As shown in Fig. 7a, when the RH level changes from 0% to 90%, the resistance of all the samples decreases whereas the resistance of GCN-Fe-Cu film sensor decreased from 9.42 kΩ to 2.96 kΩ, indicating that the composite sensor is ultra-sensitive to humidity changes. The GCN-Fe-Cu film sensor shows a sensing response of 82% in comparison to 22% for GCN and 59% for GCN-Fe composite respectively as indicated in Fig. 7b. For the fabrication of effective humidity sensing devices, the response/recovery times, long term stability and hysteresis characteristics of a humidity sensing device are highly important. The dynamic response of GCN-Fe-Cu heterostructure sensor for the change in humidity from 10 %RH to 90 %RH is shown in Fig. 7c. The GCN-Fe-Cu composite sensor shows a faster response and recovery time in comparison to pure GCN, and GCN-Fe. The GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposite sensor shows a short response and recovery time of 26 s and 19 s respectively (table-2 of supplementary information-S2). The short response and recovery time of GCN-Fe-Cu composite demonstrates faster absorption and desorption of water molecules. The response/recovery time data indicates that this GCN-Fe-Cu composite sensor is ultra-sensitive towards detection of humidity changes. Humidity dependent hysteresis behavior of GCN-Fe-Cu composite sensor is shown in Fig. 8 a. The absorption and desorption cycles show identical tracing indicating that the hysteresis of the composite sensor is negligibly small and can be ignored. Further, the GCN-Fe-Cu composite sensor demonstrates highly stable humidity sensing response for a period of 30 days @ 90 %RH as indicated in Fig. 8b. The sensitivity of GCN-Fe-Cu composite sensor shows small decrement of less than 5% in sensitivity over a period of 30 days, indicating excellent long-term stability of GCN-Fe-Cu composite sensor towards detection of humidity.

The improved humidity sensing behavior of GCN films can be attributed to the large surface area of GCN and presence of large number of active sites on the surface that can significantly improve the adsorption and desorption capabilities of water molecules on the GCN [59]. During the synthesis of GCN, the amino groups present in the sample results due to the incomplete polycondensation and introduces nitrogen defects in the system. At low relative humidity (RH), there will be formation of hydrogen bonds among the nitrogen defects of GCN, as a result the GCN surface shrinks and causes curling of the film. At the same time, the adsorbed water molecules on the sensor (GCN) surface results into formation discontinuous layers that will hinder the free movement of charges, leading to high resistance at low RH. At high RH, adsorption of water molecules on the surface of GCN film takes place in terms of multiple layers. These adsorbed water molecules form hydrogen bond with nitrogen defects, resulting the film surface to expand and hence the resistance decreases. Initially, when the small amount of water molecules is adsorbed at the active sites of GCN, and the water layers gets dispersed. When the RH increases, water molecules are adsorbed in terms of multiple layers on the film surface, that results into multilayer adsorption [60]. The presence of amino groups leads to more hydrogen bonding and continuously binding with water molecules. This adsorption process on the film surface will generate more protons (H3O+→H2O+ H+) [61]. The excessive protons generated on the film surface leads to increased proton hopping between adjacent water molecules thereby reducing the film resistance at higher RH. Also, it is known that, as per the Grotthuss chain reactions H2O + H3O+→H3O+ + H2O, when the water molecules penetrate through the intermediate layers between GCN films, the conductivity of the sensor (GCN) increases, accordingly the resistance decreases [62]. Since the presence of FeO and CuO in GCN facilitates improved conductivity due to the formation of conducting islands and ease of charge carrier hopping as discussed in conductivity section above, the GCN-Fe-Cu composite sensor shows improved humidity sensing in comparison to bare GCN.

To understand the response of fabricated sensor for different water content in the soil samples, the standard protocols to prepare the soil samples were followed [63]. Two types of soil samples namely, black clay soil and red garden soil were collected from the agricultural fields and greenhouses. These collected soil samples were dried at 110 °C for overnight to remove the moisture content, further the water is added into desired quantities (gravimetric water content) in the soil samples and kept for maturing for roughly 2 days. The gravimetric water content in the soil samples is measured as per the procedure reported in previous work [63]. The soil samples with different content of water are used to test the presence of soil moisture by inserting the fabricated sensor in the sample molds, and the change in resistance was detected during dry and wet conditions (an average of 3 trials are considered to ensure the optimization and reproducibility of the sensor characteristics) followed by evaluating the sensor response at room temperature. The sensor response for different gravimetric water content in both black clay soil and red garden soil were tested and depicted in Fig. . 9 (a, b). In both the soil samples, the GCN-Fe-Cu composite sensor shows superior performance in comparison to bare GCN and GCN-Fe composite sensor. The sensor response was found to be slightly better in black clay soil in comparison to red garden soil. The fabricated GCN-Fe-Cu composite sensor shows a response of about 72% for black clay soil against 59% for red garden soil. The improved performance of sensor response for black clay soil could be due to better adsorption of water molecules on the sensor surface in comparison to similar adsorption of water molecules on sensor surface in red garden soil.

To analyze the sensor response with and without passivation, we have used two types of sensors GCN-Fe-Cu composite sensor (without passivation) and GCN-Fe-Cu composite covered with Teflon coat (with passivation), the Teflon coat on the GCN-Fe-Cu composite surface prevents the interaction of water molecules present in the soil sample with sensor surface, hence acts as passive layer. The sensor response with and without passivation for both the soil samples is shown in Fig. 9 (c, d). The resistance of sensor with passivation slightly reduces from 16 kΩ to 13.1 kΩ whereas it reduces from 16 kΩ to 2.4 kΩ for sensor without passivation when water content changes from 0 to 50% in case of black clay soil. Similarly, for red garden soil, the resistance reduces from 16 kΩ to 7.3 kΩ for the sensor without passivation when water content changes from 0 to 50%. Further, the GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposite shows a response/recovery time of 52 s/67 s and 63 s/74 s for black and red soils respectively (table-3, supplementary information-S2). Stability of the sensor device is one of the important parameters for the practical usage of any sensor in technological applications. The GCN-Fe-Cu composite sensor shows excellent stability in terms of sensor response for a period of 30 days for both black clay and red garden soils for a gravimetric water content of 50% at room temperature as shown in Fig. 10 (a, b).

This novel GCN based FeO and CuO heterostructure nanocomposite (GCN-Fe-Cu) shows multifunctional characteristics with superior methane gas sensing @ ppb level, excellent response for changing %RH, and soil moisture sensing performance at room temperature in comparison to previously published literature [64–73] as shown in tables (1–3) [supplementary information-S2]. Hence, this GCN-Fe-Cu composite could be an excellent material for the fabrication single platform multifunctional versatile sensor in smart agricultural technologies.

In this work, we have successfully synthesized and fabricated novel multifunction sensor based on graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) filled with FeO (Iron oxide) and Copper oxide (CuO) nanoparticles to form a heterostructure composite. The structural features of these heterostructure nanocomposites were analyzed through HR-TEM, SAED, EDAX, XRD, FT-IR and XPS studies. Incorporation of FeO and CuO NPs in GCN results in enhanced conductivity of composite due to the formation of excellent conductive network. Further, the sensor device fabricated using bare GCN, GCN-Fe and GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposites are adopted as sensors for the detection methane gas @ ppb level, relative humidity (%RH) and soil moisture content. The GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposite sensor shows excellent sensitivity, short response/recovery time, superior selectivity, and long-term stability towards the detection of methane gas @ ppb level, relative humidity (%RH) and soil moisture content. This GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposite sensor acts as a single sensing platform towards detection of multiple analytes. Owing to the advanced functionalities of this hybrid nanocomposite, GCN-Fe-Cu nanocomposite sensor could be ideal to design multifunctional sensor platforms for monitoring various greenhouse conditions as well as in agricultural applications.
